participants.reasoning: The extracted participants describe only 167 screened individuals with probable dysthymia and no current MDD, whereas the official study reports 431 randomized participants with broader inclusion of major depressive disorder, persistent depressive disorder, or both.
participants.extracted: The study included 167 employed adults aged 45 years or older who screened positive for probable dysthymia and did not have current major depressive disorder, based on study screening criteria. Participants were recruited from 24 employer or employee-benefit sites in the United States and were required to have work limitations. The subgroup was predominantly female (73.7%), White non-Hispanic (87.3%), and highly educated (72.4% had at least a bachelor’s degree). Mean age was 54.6 years (SD 6.0), most worked full-time (88.6%) in white-collar occupations (73.7%), and the mean work week was 42.6 hours (SD 10.8).

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention and usual care details in content, format, duration, delivery mode, and provider characteristics, with only minor paraphrasing.
interventions.extracted: The experimental intervention was a work-focused intervention (WFI) delivered over four months as eight 50-minute telephone counseling sessions, typically twice monthly, by master’s-level counselors with employee assistance program experience. It combined three integrated components: work coaching and modification to help participants identify and change modifiable aspects of work behaviors, work processes, or work conditions; care coordination to improve communication and alignment among the participant, counselor, and health care provider regarding depression treatment, medication adherence, side effects, and work limitations; and work-focused cognitive behavioral therapy to address psychological barriers to functioning using coping skills, cognitive restructuring, behavioral homework, and workbook-based exercises adapted for work-related examples. The comparison condition was usual care, in which participants received links to depression information and care resources through their affiliated study site but no direct intervention contact.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include presenteeism, health-related absenteeism, and WLQ subscales plus PHQ-9, while the official outcomes are only sickness absence and depressive symptoms, so the outcome set does not match.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure the following outcomes: primary outcome—presenteeism, assessed by the Work Limitations Questionnaire (WLQ) at-work productivity loss (PL) score, measured at baseline and 4-month follow-up; secondary outcome—health-related absenteeism, assessed by the WLQ Time Loss Module productivity loss due to absences (APL), measured at baseline and 4-month follow-up; secondary outcome—depression symptom severity, assessed by the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), measured at baseline and 4-month follow-up. In addition, the WLQ work performance subscales used for the primary endpoint were time management, physical tasks, mental-interpersonal tasks, and output tasks, each assessed at baseline and 4-month follow-up.
